thresholds.status: Currently ~0.72; SCM substitution advancing in emerging markets

thresholds.domain: Calcium silicate cement >1% global clinker share
thresholds.status: Brimstone first plant ~103K-140K tonnes; 0.003% of global output

thresholds.domain: Carbon price >$100/tCO2 in all major cement markets
thresholds.status: EU ETS ~€65; China ETS ~$10; U.S. no federal price

thresholds.domain: DOE Brimstone grant reinstatement or equivalent
thresholds.status: Grant cancelled May 2025; administration reversal required

items.name: Calcination Necessity
items.description: Portland cement clinker requires CaCO3 (limestone) as its primary feedstock; no demonstrated alternative feedstock produces equivalent binding properties at commercial scale.

items.name: Stoichiometric Irreducibility
items.description: CaCO3 → CaO + CO2 is a conservation-of-mass identity: 100 kg of limestone yields 56 kg of lime and 44 kg of CO2. No process chemistry can eliminate this CO2 while preserving the CaO product.

items.name: Institutional Lock-in
items.description: The $330B global cement industry's infrastructure, supply chains, and capital stock are configured for limestone-based production; sovereign regulatory action cancelled the primary alternative funding stream (DOE Brimstone grant, May 2025).

methods.welfare_function: W computed via Social Cost of Carbon ($190/tCO2) applied to 2.6 GtCO2/year process emissions plus 1.1 GtCO2 combustion emissions, plus VSL-weighted mortality from PM2.5 and NOx from cement kilns.
methods.cooperative_baseline: Cement production under full CCS for process emissions plus electrified kilns, retaining $85B in essential construction material value with net-zero CO2 impact.
key_sources: Global Cement and Concrete Association, Getting the Numbers Right (2023)
falsification: F1: Demonstrate that calcium silicate cement (Brimstone process) achieves ASTM C150 compliance at >1% of global clinker output, establishing commercial-scale A1 escape.
F2: Show that CaCO3 calcination can produce binding CaO without releasing stoichiometric CO2 — i.e., refute A2 via novel process chemistry.
F3: Demonstrate that supplementary cementitious materials (fly ash, slag, calcined clay) can fully replace Portland clinker in all structural applications without strength or durability penalty.
